ucs.title: [LLM] admin@338 (G0018) post-exploitation recon burst from Office child process (PoisonIvy hands-on-keyboard)
ucs.description: Hunts the characteristic admin@338 tradecraft of dropping PoisonIvy via a weaponized Office lure tied to a current news event, then immediately running a tight cluster of native discovery commands (net, ipconfig, tasklist, systeminfo, net view) under cmd.exe spawned by WINWORD/EXCEL. This burst-of-commands pattern after Office exploitation is a documented admin@338 signature from the FireEye report.
ucs.splunk: | tstats `security_content_summariesonly` count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Processes.process) as cmdlines values(Processes.process_name) as child_procs dc(Processes.process_name) as distinct_recon_cmds from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.parent_process_name IN ("winword.exe","excel.exe","powerpnt.exe","outlook.exe") AND Processes.process_name IN ("cmd.exe","net.exe","net1.exe","ipconfig.exe","tasklist.exe","systeminfo.exe","whoami.exe","nbtstat.exe","netstat.exe","qprocess.exe","query.exe") OR (Processes.parent_process_name="cmd.exe" AND Processes.process_name IN ("net.exe","net1.exe","ipconfig.exe","tasklist.exe","systeminfo.exe","whoami.exe","nbtstat.exe","netstat.exe")) by Processes.dest Processes.user Processes.parent_process_name _time span=5m | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | where distinct_recon_cmds >= 4 | eval admin338_score=distinct_recon_cmds, recon_match=mvfilter(match(cmdlines,"(?i)(net\s+(view|user|localgroup|group|session)|ipconfig\s+/all|tasklist|systeminfo|whoami|nbtstat\s+-n)")) | where mvcount(recon_match)>=3 | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
ucs.kql:
let RECON = dynamic(["net.exe","net1.exe","ipconfig.exe","tasklist.exe","systeminfo.exe","whoami.exe","nbtstat.exe","netstat.exe","qprocess.exe","query.exe"]);
let OFFICE = dynamic(["winword.exe","excel.exe","powerpnt.exe","outlook.exe"]);
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| where (InitiatingProcessFileName in~ (OFFICE) and FileName in~ (RECON) )
   or (InitiatingProcessParentFileName in~ (OFFICE) and InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "cmd.exe" and FileName in~ (RECON))
| extend ReconCmd = tolower(strcat(FileName," ", ProcessCommandLine))
| where ReconCmd matches regex @"(net\s+(view|user|localgroup|group|session)|ipconfig\s+/all|tasklist|systeminfo|whoami|nbtstat\s+-n)"
| summarize FirstSeen=min(Timestamp), LastSeen=max(Timestamp), DistinctRecon=dcount(FileName), Cmds=make_set(ProcessCommandLine,20), Officers=make_set(InitiatingProcessParentFileName,5) by DeviceId, DeviceName, AccountName, bin(Timestamp, 10m)
| where DistinctRecon >= 4
| order by FirstSeen desc
ucs.rationale: admin@338's FireEye-documented playbook is: news-event-themed Office lure → CVE exploit / macro → PoisonIvy → operator immediately runs a near-identical recon battery (net view / net localgroup administrators / ipconfig /all / tasklist / systeminfo) within minutes. Office-spawned cmd plus a >=4-command recon burst in a 10-minute window is rare in benign telemetry and tightly fits this group's hand

ucs.title: [LLM] admin@338 PoisonIvy/Bozok masqueraded binary beaconing from user-writable paths
ucs.description: Hunts admin@338's PoisonIvy and LOWBALL/BUBBLEWRAP staging pattern: a binary masquerading as a legitimate Windows or AV process name (T1036.005) executing from %TEMP%, %APPDATA% or %ProgramData%, then making periodic outbound HTTP(S) callbacks - frequently to compromised legitimate infrastructure or free file-host C2 (a documented admin@338 trick using Dropbox/cloud staging).
ucs.splunk: | tstats `security_content_summariesonly` count values(Processes.process_path) as paths values(Processes.process_hash) as hashes from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.process_name IN ("svchost.exe","lsass.exe","explorer.exe","iexplore.exe","chrome.exe","msmpeng.exe","taskhost.exe","conhost.exe") by Processes.dest Processes.process_name Processes.process_path Processes.user | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | where NOT match(process_path,"(?i)\\\\Windows\\\\(System32|SysWOW64|explorer\\.exe)|Program Files") AND match(process_path,"(?i)\\\\(Users\\\\[^\\\\]+\\\\(AppData|Downloads|Temp)|ProgramData|Windows\\\\Temp)") | join type=inner dest [| tstats `security_content_summariesonly` count values(All_Traffic.dest_ip) as c2_ips values(All_Traffic.dest_port) as c2_ports dc(_time) as beacons from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic where All_Traffic.dest_port IN (80,443,8080,3460) AND All_Traffic.action="allowed" by All_Traffic.src _time span=1h | `drop_dm_object_name(All_Traffic)` | rename src as dest | where beacons >= 6 ] | table dest user process_name process_path hashes c2_ips c2_ports beacons
ucs.kql:
let MasqNames = dynamic(["svchost.exe","lsass.exe","explorer.exe","iexplore.exe","chrome.exe","msmpeng.exe","taskhost.exe","conhost.exe","winlogon.exe"]);
let Susp = DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| where FileName in~ (MasqNames)
| where FolderPath !startswith @"C:\Windows\System32" and FolderPath !startswith @"C:\Windows\SysWOW64" and FolderPath !startswith @"C:\Program Files"
| where FolderPath has_any (@"\AppData\", @"\Temp\", @"\Downloads\", @"\ProgramData\", @"\Windows\Temp\")
| project DeviceId, DeviceName, ProcId=ProcessId, MasqProc=FileName, MasqPath=FolderPath, SHA256, AccountName, StartTime=Timestamp;
Susp
| join kind=inner (
    DeviceNetworkEvents
    | where Timestamp > ago(7d)
    | where ActionType in ("ConnectionSuccess","HttpConnectionInspected","ConnectionRequest")
    | where RemotePort in (80,443,8080,3460)
    | summarize Beacons=count(), DistinctHours=dcount(bin(Timestamp,1h)), C2s=make_set(RemoteIP,10), URLs=make_set(RemoteUrl,10) by DeviceId, InitiatingProcessId, InitiatingProcessFileName
    | where Beacons >= 6 and DistinctHours >= 2
) on DeviceId
| where InitiatingProcessId == ProcId
| project StartTime, DeviceName, AccountName, MasqProc, MasqPath, SHA256, Beacons, DistinctHours, C2s, URLs
ucs.rationale: PoisonIvy (S0012) is admin@338's signature implant and is repeatedly observed renamed to legit Windows/AV process names and dropped to user-writable folders by their phishing payloads. Combining the masquerade-by-path heuristic with a low-volume periodic outbound beacon (PoisonIvy default ports including 3460) targets this specific implant family rather than generic LOLBins, and is well aligned wi
